method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga volcano is located on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, a subduction-related volcanic chain formed where the Pacific Plate descends beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice is mostly submarine, rising more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[4] Its summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, of which only the small islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai formerly emerged above sea level.[4]

Early geological work interpreted Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai as remnants of the rim of a large submarine caldera, based on their curved alignment around a rocky shoal that had been the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] Rock samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping gently away from the inferred caldera centre.[2] Later multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 km by 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the site, supporting the interpretation of a submarine caldera formed by collapse during a previous explosive eruption.[2] The volcano’s eruptive products are chiefly andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4]

An eruption in 2014–2015 built a large tephra cone that joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island, commonly called Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] The new island became a focus of geomorphological studies because its rapid erosion and landform evolution provided an analogue for similar features observed on Mars.[4] Before the 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum altitude of 149 m.[5]

The main eruption on 15 January 2022 destroyed most of the 2014–2015 island, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean surface.[4][5] The eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may have ranked as high as VEI 5, although the final explosivity estimate depended on more precise calculation of erupted volume.[5] It produced the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments and was the most explosive event of the satellite era.[1]

==Geography==
The site lies in Tonga’s Haʻapai island group, about 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island, and about 30 km south of the submarine volcano Fonuafoʻou.[4] Before the January 2022 eruption, the combined above-water island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of about 149 m.[4] The island was uninhabited, and its short-lived land surface was notable as a rare modern volcanic island whose erosion and morphological change could be tracked by satellite observations.[5] After the 2022 eruption, the formerly continuous island was largely removed, leaving only small remnants at the western and eastern ends of the former landmass.[4] Ash from the eruption was deposited across several parts of Tonga, with reported thicknesses of about 5–50 mm across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu, and ʻEua island groups.[4] On Tongatapu, where most of Tonga’s population lives, ashfall was reported at roughly 20–30 mm, affecting the island’s built and road surfaces.[4]

==Eruptions==
The climactic phase of the 2022 eruption occurred on 15 January at about 5 p.m. local time, when violent explosions sent gas and ash about 36 miles into the atmosphere.[4] Satellite analyses measured a vertical plume more than 30 km high, with overshooting tops above 55 km, described as a record height in the satellite era.[5] Estimates from surface-pressure data put the energy release of the initial explosion at 10–28 exajoules, probably greater than the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly comparable to the 1883 eruption of Krakatoa.[5]

The eruption was interpreted as an unusually powerful phreatomagmatic event caused by interaction between seawater and magma at temperatures above 1,000 °C.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin and colleagues described the event as unlike a standard Surtseyan eruption because of the very large volume of water involved, informally calling it an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption.[3] One proposed mechanism was weakening and partial collapse of part of the caldera rim, which may have allowed large volumes of seawater to enter an underground magma chamber.[3] The absence of seismometers on the volcano, and the fact that the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles away, limited direct observation of the processes leading up to the eruption.[4] In response, the United States Geological Survey developed methods to identify earthquakes near the volcano that its automated systems had not detected, producing a near-real-time catalogue of post-eruption seismicity.[4]

The eruption generated tsunamis that reached up to 65 ft above sea level on Tongan islands, destroying hundreds of structures, displacing more than 1,500 people, and killing four people in Tonga.[4] Tsunami waves also crossed the Pacific, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and producing damage or impacts in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Subsequent seafloor surveys found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples up to 50 km from the volcano, together with gouged valleys and large sediment deposits.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed during the eruption and was later found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4] Scientists estimated that as much as 1⅔ cubic miles of material had been displaced, with additional deposits likely remaining unmapped.[4]

The eruption injected large quantities of volcanic aerosols and water vapour into the stratosphere, including an unprecedented amount of water vapour for a submarine eruption observed by modern satellites.[2] The eruption increased total stratospheric water content by about 10%, while also emitting sulfur dioxide that was converted into sulfate aerosols.[1][2] Because water vapour and sulfate aerosols have opposing climate effects, the eruption initially raised questions about whether it would warm or cool the climate system.[1] Later studies found that the net effect from 2022 was slight cooling, including an estimated 0.1 °C reduction over the Southern Hemisphere, mainly because sulfate particles reflected sunlight back to space.[1] Some analyses nevertheless indicated a possible small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere, where stratospheric water vapour can persist for years.[1]

==Footnotes==
Scientific datasets from later studies of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai have been archived in public repositories, including PANGAEA and Figshare, with supplementary material also made available by the publisher.[1]

Post-eruption research emphasized that the event has implications for interpreting both historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis, especially because volcanic, atmospheric, and oceanic processes may interact in ways not fully captured by conventional tsunami models.[3]

Some early expert commentary proposed that flank collapse or submarine landsliding may have helped make the eruption unusually explosive, reflecting the instability of emerging island volcanoes.[2]

Subsequent climate-focused studies reported differing stratospheric effects, including strong mid-stratospheric cooling and lower-stratospheric warming linked to the injection of water vapour.[4]

Other analyses concluded that the eruption produced a small net cooling over the Southern Hemisphere, attributing the effect to the combined radiative influence of sulfate aerosols, water vapour, ozone changes, and particle size.[5]
